does: This paper introduces Gated Sparse Autoencoders, a modified architecture for discovering interpretable features in language model activations. The core innovation separates sparsity control into two components: a gating mechanism that determines which feature directions activate, and a magnitude estimation that scales those activations. By applying the L1 penalty only to the gating function rather than directly to feature magnitudes, the method eliminates shrinkage bias where feature activations are systematically underestimated. Empirical evaluation across language models up to 7B parameters demonstrates that Gated SAEs achieve Pareto improvements over standard sparse autoencoders, requiring half as many active features for equivalent reconstruction quality while maintaining interpretability and eliminating the magnitude estimation distortions inherent to L1-penalized methods.
connects: This work builds directly on recent advances in using sparse autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability of neural networks, particularly the application to unsupervised feature discovery in language model activations. The paper addresses a fundamental technical limitation of standard SAEs: the L1 regularization technique commonly used to induce sparsity introduces unwanted side effects including shrinkage bias. The gating mechanism draws on broader ideas from neural network architecture design where separate pathways handle different computational functions, similar to gating in recurrent networks and mixture-of-experts models. The motivation connects to ongoing efforts to make deep learning systems more interpretable and to understand which directions in activation space correspond to meaningful semantic or functional features.
enables: Gated SAEs create foundations for more efficient and accurate mechanistic interpretability research by providing cleaner feature representations with reduced artifacts. The improved efficiency—requiring fewer active features—suggests potential applications to larger models and real-time feature attribution tasks. Better magnitude estimation opens possibilities for more faithful causal analysis of feature contributions to model behavior. The work enables downstream applications like steering language models through feature activation and building more reliable safety monitors based on interpretable features. By reducing the computational footprint of feature discovery, it facilitates scaling interpretability techniques to frontier-scale models and enables more rigorous investigation of feature geometry and interactions.
